analogy: Think of "Control and Coordination" through the chapter's own opening images: 7 CHAPTER Before we discuss the mechanisms by which organisms reproduce, let us ask a more basic question – why do organisms reproduce? After all, reproduction is not necessary to maintain the life of an individual organism, unlike the essential life processes such as nutrition,... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Control and Coordination is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 7 CHAPTER Before we discuss the mechanisms by which organisms reproduce, let us ask a more basic question – why do organisms reproduce?

The text explains that after all, reproduction is not necessary to maintain the life of an individual organism, unlike the essential life processes such as nutrition, respiration, or excretion.

The text explains that on the other hand, if an individual organism is going to create more individuals, a lot of its energy will be spent in the process.

The text explains that so why should an individual organism waste energy on a process it does not need to stay alive?

The text explains that it would be interesting to discuss the possible answers in the classroom!

The text explains that whatever the answer to this question, it is obvious that we notice organisms because they reproduce.

The text explains that if there were to be only one, non- reproducing member of a particular kind, it is doubtful that we would have noticed its existence.

The text explains that it is the large numbers of organisms belonging to a single species that bring them to our notice.

The text explains that how do we know that two different individual organisms belong to the same species?

The text explains that usually, we say this because they look similar to each other.

The text explains that thus, reproducing organisms create new individuals that look very much like themselves.

The text explains that 7.1 DO ORG 7.1 DO ORG 7.1 DO ORG 7.1 DO ORG 7.1 DO ORGANISMS CREA ANISMS CREA ANISMS CREA ANISMS CREA ANISMS CREATE EXA TE EXA TE EXA TE EXA TE EXACT COPIES OF CT COPIES OF CT COPIES OF CT COPIES OF CT COPIES OF THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSELVES?

The text explains that organisms look similar because their body designs are similar.

The text explains that if body designs are to be similar, the blueprints for these designs should be similar.

The text explains that thus, reproduction at its most basic level will involve making copies of the blueprints of body design.

The text explains that in Class IX, we learnt that the chromosomes in the nucleus of a cell contain information for inheritance of features from parents to next generation in the form of DNA (Deoxyribo Nucleic Acid) molecules.

The text explains that the DNA in the cell nucleus is the information source for making proteins.

The text explains that temperatures on earth can go up or down, water levels can vary, or there could be meteorite hits, to think of a few examples.

The text explains that thus, if there were a population of bacteria living in temperate waters, and if the water temperature were to be increased by global warming, most of these bacteria would die, but the few variants resistant to heat would survive and grow further.

The text explains that the DNA copying mechanism, as we have noted, cannot be absolutely accurate, and the resultant errors are a source of variations in populations of organisms.

The text explains that however, while the body of the individual organism is growing to its adult size, the resources of the body are mainly directed at achieving this growth.

The text explains that these are located outside the abdominal cavity in scrotum because sperm formation requires a lower temperature than the normal body temperature.

The text explains that all of us are under many different kinds of pressures about these issues.

The text explains that there can be pressure from our friends for participating in many activities, whether we really want to or not.

The text explains that there can be pressure from families to get married and start having children.

The text explains that there can be pressure from government agencies to avoid having children.

The text explains that asexual reproduction takes place through budding in (a) Amoeba.

The text explains that which of the following is not a part of the female reproductive system in human beings?

The text explains that (a) Ovary (b) Uterus (c) Vas deferens (d) Fallopian tube 3.

The text explains that what are the advantages of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?

The text explains that what are the functions performed by the testis in human beings?

The text explains that draw a labelled diagram of the longitudinal section of a flower.

The text explains that what are the different methods of contraception?

The text explains that how are the modes for reproduction different in unicellular and multicellular organisms?

The text explains that how does reproduction help in providing stability to populations of species?

The text explains that what could be the reasons for adopting contraceptive methods?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 7: Control and Coordination

revision_bullets: 7 CHAPTER Before we discuss the mechanisms by which organisms reproduce, let us ask a more basic question – why do organisms reproduce? (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)

After all, reproduction is not necessary to maintain the life of an individual organism, unlike the essential life processes such as nutrition, respiration, or excretion. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
On the other hand, if an individual organism is going to create more individuals, a lot of its energy will be spent in the process. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
So why should an individual organism waste energy on a process it does not need to stay alive? (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
It would be interesting to discuss the possible answers in the classroom! (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
Whatever the answer to this question, it is obvious that we notice organisms because they reproduce. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
If there were to be only one, non- reproducing member of a particular kind, it is doubtful that we would have noticed its existence. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
It is the large numbers of organisms belonging to a single species that bring them to our notice. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
How do we know that two different individual organisms belong to the same species? (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)
Usually, we say this because they look similar to each other. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch7 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 7 CHAPTER and THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSELVES? VES? VES? VES? VES? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Control and Coordination; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Control and Coordination through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 7 CHAPTER and THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSEL THEMSELVES? VES? VES? VES? VES? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
